sections.title: Identity & Access Management — Privilege Escalation & Over-Privileged Roles

items.title: Wildcard actions and resources in IAM policies
items.description: Policies containing "Action": "*" or "Resource": "*" (AWS), Owner/Contributor at subscription scope (Azure), or roles/owner and primitive roles (GCP) grant excessive access that violates least privilege.
items.how_to_find:
Find policies granting `*` actions/resources:

```
aws iam list-policies --scope Local --query 'Policies[].Arn' --output text | while read a; do v=$(aws iam get-policy --policy-arn $a --query 'Policy.DefaultVersionId' --output text); aws iam get-policy-version --policy-arn $a --version-id $v --query "PolicyVersion.Document.Statement[?Action=='*'||Resource=='*']"; done
```

Automated: Prowler / ScoutSuite / `cloudsplaining`. `Action:*` on `Resource:*` = high.
items.how_to_fix: Replace wildcards with least-privilege actions scoped to specific ARNs; use IAM Access Analyzer to generate policies from CloudTrail activity, add conditions, and remove admin-equivalent grants. Re-scan.

items.title: Known IAM privilege-escalation paths
items.description: Permissions such as iam:CreatePolicyVersion, iam:PassRole + ec2:RunInstances, lambda:UpdateFunctionCode, or sts:AssumeRole chains can be abused to escalate from a low-privileged identity to admin.
items.how_to_find:
Enumerate escalation paths (e.g. `iam:CreatePolicyVersion`, `iam:PassRole+lambda`, `iam:AttachUserPolicy`):

```
pmapper graph create && pmapper query 'preset privesc *'
# or: cloudsplaining scan --input-file account.json
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove the specific escalation-enabling permissions from non-admin principals, separate duties, require permission boundaries, and monitor sensitive IAM actions. Re-run pmapper to confirm paths are closed.

items.title: iam:PassRole and actAs abuse
items.description: An identity allowed to pass a more privileged role to a compute service (EC2, Lambda, ECS, GCE, Cloud Functions) can assume that role indirectly and inherit its permissions.
items.how_to_find:
Find principals that can pass/act-as powerful roles (a privesc primitive):

```
# AWS: who has iam:PassRole with broad resource
cloudsplaining scan ...   # flags PassRole
# GCP: who has iam.serviceAccounts.actAs
gcloud iam service-accounts get-iam-policy <sa>
```
items.how_to_fix: Scope `iam:PassRole`/`actAs` to specific roles/service accounts only, add conditions (e.g. `iam:PassedToService`), and never grant it broadly. Re-audit.

items.title: Unused and stale permissions
items.description: Permissions and identities granted but never used widen the attack surface.
items.how_to_find:
Identify granted-but-unused permissions:

```
aws accessanalyzer list-findings --analyzer-arn <arn>
aws iam generate-service-last-accessed-details --arn <principal-arn>
```
items.how_to_fix: Right-size policies to actually-used services/actions (Access Analyzer last-accessed data), remove unused grants, and review regularly. Adopt least privilege as default.

sections.title: Identity & Access Management — Access Keys & Long-Lived Credentials

items.title: Leaked access keys in code, repos, and artifacts
items.description: AWS access keys, Azure client secrets, and GCP service account JSON keys are frequently committed to source control, container images, CI logs, and public buckets.
items.how_to_find:
Scan repos, images, and artifacts for cloud keys:

```
gitleaks detect --source . --redact
trufflehog filesystem ./ --only-verified
grep -REo 'AKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}' .
```
items.how_to_fix: Rotate/revoke leaked keys immediately, remove from history (BFG/filter-repo), move to short-lived roles (STS/OIDC/workload identity), and add secret scanning as a blocking CI gate + push protection. Re-scan.

items.title: Long-lived static credentials
items.description: Static keys without expiry (AWS IAM user keys, Azure SP secrets, GCP SA keys) should be replaced by short-lived roles, workload identity federation, or instance/managed identities.
items.how_to_find:
List static access keys and their age:

```
aws iam list-users --query 'Users[].UserName' --output text | tr '\t' '\n' | while read u; do aws iam list-access-keys --user-name $u --query 'AccessKeyMetadata[].[UserName,CreateDate,Status]' --output text; done
```
items.how_to_fix: Replace static keys with short-lived credentials (IAM roles, STS, OIDC federation, workload identity). Where keys are unavoidable, rotate frequently and scope tightly. Re-audit for remaining static keys.

references.title: GCP service account key best practices

items.title: Missing or stale key rotation
items.description: Credentials not rotated within policy windows increase the impact of a leak.
items.how_to_find:
Flag keys older than policy (e.g. 90 days):

```
aws iam get-credential-report --output text --query Content | base64 -d | awk -F, 'NR>1{print $1,$9,$10,$11}'
```
items.how_to_fix: Enforce automatic rotation (SDK/Secrets Manager), disable keys past max age via SCP/config rule, and prefer role-based short-lived creds. Re-check the credential report.

items.title: Active credentials on inactive or dormant identities
items.description: Service accounts and users that are no longer used but still hold active keys are prime targets for credential-stuffing and reuse.
items.how_to_find:
Find enabled credentials on unused identities:

```
aws iam get-credential-report --output text --query Content | base64 -d | awk -F, 'NR>1{print $1,$5,$12}'   # password_last_used, access_key_last_used
```
items.how_to_fix: Disable/delete credentials for dormant identities, remove unused accounts, and enforce an access-review + automatic-deactivation process for inactivity. Re-audit.

references.title: MITRE ATT&CK - Valid Accounts: Cloud Accounts

sections.title: Identity & Access Management — MFA & Root/Privileged Account Hygiene

items.title: Missing MFA on console/privileged users
items.description: Human identities with console access and no MFA are vulnerable to phishing and password reuse.
items.how_to_find:
List console users without MFA:

```
aws iam get-credential-report --output text --query Content | base64 -d | awk -F, 'NR>1 && $4=="true" && $8=="false"{print $1}'
```

Console access without MFA = high finding.
items.how_to_fix: Require MFA for all console/privileged users (enforce via SCP/Conditional Access), prefer phishing-resistant factors (FIDO2), and block access without MFA. Re-check the report.

items.title: Root / Global Admin account usage
items.description: The AWS root account, Azure Global Administrator, and GCP super admin should be locked down with MFA, no access keys, and used only for break-glass scenarios.
items.how_to_find:
Detect root/Global Admin sign-ins and API use:

```
aws cloudtrail lookup-events --lookup-attributes AttributeKey=Username,AttributeValue=root --max-results 20
```

Any routine root/GA usage = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Lock away root/Global Admin (hardware MFA, no keys), use it only for break-glass with alerting, and perform daily ops via least-privilege roles. Alert on any root usage. Re-verify no routine use.

items.title: Weak MFA factors on highly privileged identities
items.description: SMS and TOTP can be phished; phishing-resistant factors (FIDO2/hardware keys) are recommended for break-glass and admin identities.
items.how_to_find:
Check MFA factor types on privileged identities (SMS/phone are phishable):

```
aws iam list-mfa-devices --user-name <admin>
# Azure/GCP: review registered methods per privileged user
```
items.how_to_fix: Require phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2/WebAuthn/hardware) for admins, remove SMS/voice as a factor for privileged roles, and enforce via Conditional Access / SCP. Re-audit factors.

items.title: Access keys present on root/super-admin
items.description: Root and super-admin accounts should never have programmatic access keys; their existence drastically increases blast radius if leaked.
items.how_to_find:
Check for root access keys (should be zero):

```
aws iam get-account-summary --query 'SummaryMap.AccountAccessKeysPresent'
```

`1` = high finding.
items.how_to_fix: Delete all root access keys, operate via IAM roles, and secure root with hardware MFA for break-glass only. Re-check the account summary returns `0`.

sections.title: Identity & Access Management — Trust Policies & Federation

items.title: Over-broad assume-role trust policies
items.description: Trust policies allowing Principal "*" or entire external accounts to assume a role can let attackers pivot into the account.
items.how_to_find:
Find roles trusting `*` or whole accounts without conditions:

```
aws iam list-roles --query 'Roles[?AssumeRolePolicyDocument.Statement[?Principal==`*`]].RoleName'
aws iam list-roles --query 'Roles[].[RoleName,AssumeRolePolicyDocument]'
```
items.how_to_fix: Scope trust policies to specific principals, add conditions (`aws:PrincipalOrgID`, `sts:ExternalId`, source account), and remove `Principal:*`. Re-audit trust documents.

items.title: Confused deputy via missing ExternalId / condition
items.description: Cross-account roles assumed by third parties must enforce sts:ExternalId or source conditions to prevent a confused-deputy attack.
items.how_to_find:
Check third-party/cross-account roles for an `ExternalId` condition:

```
aws iam get-role --role-name <role> --query 'Role.AssumeRolePolicyDocument.Statement[].Condition'
```

Cross-account assume without `sts:ExternalId` (or equivalent) = confused-deputy risk.
items.how_to_fix: Require a unique, secret `ExternalId` (or `aws:SourceArn`/`SourceAccount`) on every cross-account/third-party trust, and validate it. Re-check the trust policy enforces the condition.

items.title: Misconfigured OIDC / workload identity federation
items.description: GitHub Actions OIDC, GCP Workload Identity Federation, and Azure federated credentials with overly broad subject/audience claims let untrusted repos or workloads assume cloud roles.
items.how_to_find:
Review OIDC trust conditions (over-broad `sub`/`aud`, wildcard repo/branch):

```
aws iam get-role --role-name <gha-role> --query 'Role.AssumeRolePolicyDocument'
# look for token.actions.githubusercontent.com:sub with '*'
```

Wildcard `sub` lets any repo/branch assume the role = high.
items.how_to_fix: Pin the OIDC `sub` to exact repo+branch/environment (and correct `aud`), avoid wildcards, and scope the role minimally. For GCP/Azure federation, restrict the attribute mapping/condition. Re-verify the condition is specific.

items.title: Excessive service principal / managed identity grants
items.description: Azure service principals and managed identities, and GCP service accounts, with broad directory or project roles can be abused for lateral movement and persistence.
items.how_to_find:
Enumerate service principal / managed identity role assignments:

```
# Azure
az role assignment list --assignee <sp-id> -o table
# GCP
gcloud projects get-iam-policy <proj> --flatten=bindings --filter='bindings.members:serviceAccount'
```
items.how_to_fix: Right-size non-human identities to least privilege, remove Owner/Contributor where narrower roles suffice, avoid subscription/project-wide grants, and review regularly. Re-audit assignments.

items.title: Public-read buckets / containers
items.description: S3 buckets, Azure blob containers, and GCS buckets granting read to AllUsers/anonymous expose data to anyone on the internet.
items.how_to_find:
Enumerate public-read object storage:

```
# AWS
aws s3api get-bucket-policy-status --bucket <b> --query 'PolicyStatus.IsPublic'
aws s3api get-public-access-block --bucket <b>
# Azure
az storage container list --account-name <acct> --query "[?properties.publicAccess!=null]"
# GCP
gsutil iam get gs://<b> | grep allUsers
```
items.how_to_fix: Enable account-level public-access-block (AWS) / disable anonymous access (Azure `allowBlobPublicAccess=false`) / remove `allUsers`/`allAuthenticatedUsers` (GCP). Serve public content via CDN with signed URLs. Re-check public status.

items.title: Public-write / world-writable buckets
items.description: Buckets allowing anonymous PUT/DELETE enable defacement, malware hosting, and supply-chain attacks on assets served from the bucket.
items.how_to_find:
Test for anonymous write (critical — attackers can plant/modify content):

```
aws s3api get-bucket-acl --bucket <b> --query "Grants[?Grantee.URI=='http://acs.amazonaws.com/groups/global/AllUsers']"
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove all public-write ACLs/policies immediately, enable public-access-block, audit for planted objects, and restrict writes to specific principals. Re-verify no anonymous write.

items.title: Overly permissive bucket resource policies
items.description: Resource policies with wildcard principals or broad cross-account access can expose data even when ACLs appear restricted.
items.how_to_find:
Review bucket policies for `Principal:*` or broad cross-account access:

```
aws s3api get-bucket-policy --bucket <b> --query Policy --output text | python3 -m json.tool | grep -A2 Principal
```
items.how_to_fix: Scope bucket policies to specific principals with conditions (`aws:PrincipalOrgID`, `aws:SourceVpce`), deny insecure transport, and remove `*`. Re-audit the policy.

items.title: Dangling / claimable bucket names
items.description: References to deleted buckets (in code, DNS, or web assets) can be re-registered by attackers to serve malicious content (bucket takeover/squatting).
items.how_to_find:
Find references to buckets that no longer exist (claimable by attackers for takeover):

```
# grep app config/DNS/CDN origins for bucket names, then:
aws s3api head-bucket --bucket <name> 2>&1   # NoSuchBucket => claimable
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove references to deleted buckets, reclaim critical names, and avoid predictable names. For static-site/CDN origins, ensure the backing bucket exists and is owned by you. Re-check references.

references.title: Hacking the Cloud - S3 bucket takeover

sections.title: Storage — Encryption at Rest & Key Management

items.title: Unencrypted storage at rest
items.description: Buckets, disks, and databases without encryption at rest fail most compliance baselines and increase impact of physical or backup compromise.
items.how_to_find:
Find unencrypted storage:

```
aws s3api get-bucket-encryption --bucket <b> 2>&1
aws ec2 describe-volumes --query "Volumes[?Encrypted==`false`].VolumeId"
```
items.how_to_fix: Enable default encryption (SSE-KMS for buckets, EBS encryption-by-default), re-create/re-encrypt unencrypted resources, and enforce via config rules/SCP. Re-scan.

items.title: Weak KMS key policies and shared keys
items.description: KMS/Key Vault key policies with wildcard principals, or use of a single shared key across sensitive datasets, weaken cryptographic isolation.
items.how_to_find:
Review key policies for public/broad access and cross-account sharing:

```
aws kms get-key-policy --key-id <k> --policy-name default --query Policy --output text | python3 -m json.tool
```

`Principal:*` or broad `kms:*` grants are high findings.
items.how_to_fix: Scope key policies to specific roles + operations, add `aws:PrincipalOrgID`, separate key admins from users, and avoid one shared key for everything (per-workload keys). Re-audit.

items.title: Missing key rotation
items.description: KMS/Key Vault keys without automatic rotation increase exposure window if a key is compromised.
items.how_to_find:
Check automatic rotation on customer-managed keys:

```
aws kms get-key-rotation-status --key-id <k> --query KeyRotationEnabled
```
items.how_to_fix: Enable automatic annual rotation (`aws kms enable-key-rotation`); for Azure Key Vault/GCP KMS set a rotation policy. Rotate imported material on schedule. Re-check status.

items.title: Encryption in transit not enforced
items.description: Storage that allows plaintext HTTP access (no aws:SecureTransport / HTTPS-only) risks interception of data in transit.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether storage requires TLS:

```
aws s3api get-bucket-policy --bucket <b> --query Policy --output text | grep -i securetransport
# Azure: supportsHttpsTrafficOnly ; GCP: enforce TLS at LB/app
```
items.how_to_fix: Add a deny-in-clear policy (`aws:SecureTransport=false`) on buckets, set Azure `supportsHttpsTrafficOnly=true`, and enforce TLS on all endpoints. Re-verify plaintext is denied.

items.title: Pre-signed / SAS URL abuse
items.description: Long-lived or over-scoped pre-signed URLs (S3), SAS tokens (Azure), and signed URLs (GCS) can grant broad, hard-to-revoke access if leaked.
items.how_to_find:
Review pre-signed/SAS URL generation for long expiry, broad scope, or leakage:

```
# inspect app code generating presigned URLs; check SAS token expiry/permissions
```

Long-lived or over-permissioned signed URLs are high findings.
items.how_to_fix: Issue short-lived, least-privilege signed URLs/SAS tokens (specific object + verb), prefer user-delegation SAS (Azure) tied to identity, and never log/leak them. Rotate signing keys. Re-review generation logic.

items.title: Object listing enabled
items.description: Buckets that allow listing let attackers enumerate all object keys, turning a single leak into full data discovery.
items.how_to_find:
Test whether object listing is permitted:

```
curl -s https://<bucket>.s3.amazonaws.com/    # ListBucket via anonymous
aws s3 ls s3://<b> --no-sign-request
```
items.how_to_fix: Deny anonymous `s3:ListBucket`, disable directory-style listing, and separate listing permission from read. Re-test listing is refused.

items.title: Sensitive data in object versions / backups
items.description: Deleted or rotated secrets may persist in object version history, snapshots, and lifecycle-managed backups accessible to over-privileged principals.
items.how_to_find:
Check for sensitive data lingering in old versions/backups: ``` aws s3api list-object-versions --bucket --prefix

--query 'Versions[].[Key,VersionId]' ```
items.how_to_fix: Apply lifecycle policies to expire old versions, encrypt versions/backups, restrict access, and purge secrets from history (rotate them). Re-audit versions.

items.title: IMDSv1 enabled (no token requirement)
items.description: IMDSv1 responds to simple GET requests, so any SSRF on the instance can read metadata and steal role credentials.
items.how_to_find:
Find instances allowing IMDSv1 (token optional):

```
aws ec2 describe-instances --query "Reservations[].Instances[?MetadataOptions.HttpTokens=='optional'].InstanceId" --output text
```
items.how_to_fix: Require IMDSv2 (`HttpTokens=required`) on all instances and in launch templates/ASGs; set hop limit to 1. Enforce via SCP/config rule. Re-check no instance allows IMDSv1.

items.title: Role credential theft via metadata SSRF
items.description: Reaching /latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/ (AWS), the Azure IMDS token endpoint, or the GCP metadata endpoint exposes temporary credentials usable from anywhere.
items.how_to_find:
Via an app SSRF, attempt to read instance role creds:

```
curl -s "http://<app>/fetch?url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/"
```

Returned temporary creds = critical.
items.how_to_fix: Require IMDSv2 + hop-limit 1, fix the SSRF (allowlist/validate URLs, block link-local), scope the instance role minimally, and monitor STS usage. Rotate exposed creds. Re-test SSRF can't reach metadata.

items.title: Azure/GCP metadata header bypass exposure
items.description: Azure IMDS requires the Metadata:true header and GCP requires Metadata-Flavor:Google; SSRF that can set arbitrary headers can still reach them.
items.how_to_find:
Test metadata reachable via SSRF on Azure/GCP (they require a header, but SSRF may set it):

```
curl -s "http://<app>/fetch?url=http://169.254.169.254/metadata/instance?api-version=2021-02-01" -H 'Metadata: true'   # Azure
curl -s "http://<app>/fetch?url=http://metadata.google.internal/computeMetadata/v1/" -H 'Metadata-Flavor: Google'   # GCP
```
items.how_to_fix: Fix the SSRF (strip attacker-controlled headers, allowlist destinations, block link-local/metadata IPs), use least-privilege managed identities/service accounts, and monitor token issuance. Re-test.

items.title: IMDSv2 hop limit allows container access
items.description: A metadata hop limit greater than 1 lets containers on the host reach IMDS, expanding the SSRF/credential-theft surface to compromised containers.
items.how_to_find:
Check the metadata hop limit (>1 lets containers reach the node's role):

```
aws ec2 describe-instances --query "Reservations[].Instances[].MetadataOptions.HttpPutResponseHopLimit"
```

`>1` on container hosts is a finding.
items.how_to_fix: Set the hop limit to 1 on container hosts and use pod/workload identity (IRSA / Workload Identity) instead of the node role. Block pod egress to the metadata IP. Re-check the hop limit.

sections.title: Compute & Metadata — Instance Profiles & User-Data Secrets

items.title: Over-privileged instance profiles / identities
items.description: Instance profiles (AWS), managed identities (Azure), and attached service accounts (GCP) with broad permissions become a powerful pivot if the host is compromised.
items.how_to_find:
Review the instance role's attached policies:

```
aws ec2 describe-instances --query 'Reservations[].Instances[].IamInstanceProfile.Arn'
aws iam list-attached-role-policies --role-name <role>
```
items.how_to_fix: Scope instance roles to least privilege (specific actions/resources), avoid admin policies, and use separate roles per workload. Re-audit attached policies.

items.title: Secrets in user-data / startup scripts
items.description: Passwords, API keys, and tokens placed in EC2 user-data, Azure custom data, or GCP startup-script metadata are readable by any process that can reach the metadata service.
items.how_to_find:
Read instance user-data for embedded secrets:

```
aws ec2 describe-instance-attribute --instance-id <id> --attribute userData --query 'UserData.Value' --output text | base64 -d
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove secrets from user-data; fetch them at boot from Secrets Manager/Parameter Store/Key Vault using the instance identity. Rotate exposed secrets. Re-check user-data is clean.

items.title: Secrets in instance/container environment
items.description: Credentials injected as environment variables on instances and containers can leak via debug endpoints, process listing, or compromised dependencies.
items.how_to_find:
Inspect env/config for plaintext secrets:

```
aws ecs describe-task-definition --task-definition <td> --query 'taskDefinition.containerDefinitions[].environment'
```
items.how_to_fix: Inject secrets from a secrets manager at runtime (ECS `secrets`, K8s CSI, Key Vault refs), not plain env. Rotate exposed values. Re-audit task/pod definitions.

items.title: Remote-access / agent paths to instances
items.description: SSM Session Manager (AWS), Azure Bastion/Run Command, and GCP OS Login/serial console can grant interactive access; over-broad permission to these is a stealthy compromise path.
items.how_to_find:
Enumerate remote-access paths (SSH/RDP open, SSM, serial console):

```
aws ec2 describe-security-groups --query "SecurityGroups[?IpPermissions[?FromPort==`22`]]"
aws ssm describe-instance-information
```
items.how_to_fix: Prefer agent-based access (SSM Session Manager) over open SSH/RDP, close inbound management ports, require MFA/bastion, and log sessions. Re-scan for open management access.

sections.title: Compute & Metadata — Public Images, Snapshots & Disks

items.title: Public AMIs / shared images
items.description: AMIs (AWS), images (GCP), and VM images (Azure) shared publicly or with external accounts may contain baked-in secrets, source code, or configuration.
items.how_to_find:
List images you've made public:

```
aws ec2 describe-images --owners self --query "Images[?Public==`true`].[ImageId,Name]" --output table
```

Public images may contain baked-in secrets/data.
items.how_to_fix: Make images private (`modify-image-attribute --launch-permission Remove=[{Group=all}]`), scan for secrets before publishing, and share to specific accounts only. Rotate anything exposed. Re-check no public images.

items.title: Public EBS / disk snapshots
items.description: Publicly shared EBS snapshots, Azure managed disk snapshots, and GCP disk snapshots can be mounted by attackers to extract data and credentials.
items.how_to_find:
Find public snapshots (critical — full disk contents):

```
aws ec2 describe-snapshots --owner-ids self --restorable-by-user-ids all --query 'Snapshots[].SnapshotId'
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove public share (`modify-snapshot-attribute --attribute createVolumePermission --operation-type remove --group-names all`), enable snapshot block-public-access, and rotate exposed data. Re-verify none are public.

items.title: Snapshots shared with untrusted accounts
items.description: Snapshots and images shared with specific external accounts can be exfiltrated if those accounts are compromised or no longer trusted.
items.how_to_find:
List accounts each snapshot is shared with:

```
aws ec2 describe-snapshot-attribute --snapshot-id <s> --attribute createVolumePermission
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove shares to unknown accounts, share only to specific trusted account IDs with justification, and prefer encrypted-snapshot sharing (with CMK grants). Re-audit shares.

items.title: Unencrypted snapshots and volumes
items.description: Unencrypted volumes and snapshots increase impact when shared or copied, since data is readable without keys.
items.how_to_find:
Find unencrypted snapshots/volumes:

```
aws ec2 describe-snapshots --owner-ids self --query "Snapshots[?Encrypted==`false`].SnapshotId"
aws ec2 describe-volumes --query "Volumes[?Encrypted==`false`].VolumeId"
```
items.how_to_fix: Enable encryption-by-default, copy snapshots to encrypted (CMK) copies and delete unencrypted originals, and re-create volumes from encrypted snapshots. Re-scan.

items.title: Management ports open to 0.0.0.0/0
items.description: SSH (22), RDP (3389), and admin ports exposed to the entire internet via security groups, NSGs, or VPC firewall rules invite brute-force and exploitation.
items.how_to_find:
Find SSH/RDP/WinRM open to the internet:

```
aws ec2 describe-security-groups --query "SecurityGroups[?IpPermissions[?(FromPort==`22`||FromPort==`3389`) && IpRanges[?CidrIp=='0.0.0.0/0']]].GroupId"
```
items.how_to_fix: Restrict management ports to a bastion/VPN or specific admin CIDRs, prefer SSM/agent access, and remove `0.0.0.0/0` rules. Enforce via config rule. Re-scan.

items.title: Databases / data services exposed publicly
items.description: Database ports (3306, 5432, 1433, 27017, 6379) and managed data services reachable from the internet are high-value targets and frequent breach causes.
items.how_to_find:
Find DB/data ports open to the world (critical):

```
aws ec2 describe-security-groups --query "SecurityGroups[?IpPermissions[?FromPort==`3306`||FromPort==`5432`||FromPort==`27017`||FromPort==`6379` && IpRanges[?CidrIp=='0.0.0.0/0']]].GroupId"
```
items.how_to_fix: Move data services to private subnets, restrict SGs/NSGs to app tiers only, disable public accessibility on managed DBs, and use private endpoints. Rotate any exposed data. Re-scan from the internet.

items.title: Overly broad allow rules
items.description: Rules permitting all ports/protocols from wide ranges undermine segmentation and make lateral movement easy after a single compromise.
items.how_to_find:
Look for wide port ranges / large CIDRs:

```
aws ec2 describe-security-groups --query "SecurityGroups[].IpPermissions[?ToPort-FromPort>`100` || IpRanges[?CidrIp=='0.0.0.0/0']]"
```
items.how_to_fix: Tighten rules to specific ports + minimal source CIDRs, remove `0-65535`/`/0` allows, and document each rule. Use security-group references instead of CIDRs for east-west. Re-audit.

items.title: Use of default / permissive network groups
items.description: Default security groups and unrestricted default firewall rules often carry permissive settings and should not be used for production workloads.
items.how_to_find:
Check for resources using the default SG/NSG (often permissive):

```
aws ec2 describe-instances --query "Reservations[].Instances[?SecurityGroups[?GroupName=='default']].InstanceId"
```
items.how_to_fix: Don't use the default SG for workloads; create purpose-built least-privilege groups, and lock the default SG to deny-all. Re-check nothing uses a permissive default.

items.title: Over-broad VPC peering / VNet connections
items.description: Wide peering or hub-spoke designs without route filtering can let a compromise in one network reach many others.
items.how_to_find:
Review peering connections and their route/ACL scope:

```
aws ec2 describe-vpc-peering-connections --query 'VpcPeeringConnections[].[VpcPeeringConnectionId,Status.Code]'
```

Peering that exposes whole VPCs to each other is a finding.
items.how_to_fix: Limit peering to required CIDRs via route tables + SGs/NACLs (peering itself isn't filtered), prefer Transit Gateway with segmentation, and remove unused peerings. Re-audit routes.

items.title: Missing private endpoints for managed services
items.description: Accessing managed services over public endpoints instead of PrivateLink/Private Endpoint/Private Service Connect exposes traffic and broadens attack surface.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether managed-service traffic traverses the public internet vs private endpoints:

```
aws ec2 describe-vpc-endpoints --query 'VpcEndpoints[].[ServiceName,VpcEndpointType]'
```
items.how_to_fix: Use VPC/private endpoints (PrivateLink, Azure Private Endpoint, GCP Private Service Connect) for S3/KMS/databases, restrict service access to the endpoint, and block public paths. Re-verify traffic is private.

items.title: Unrestricted egress / data exfiltration paths
items.description: Unrestricted outbound traffic enables data exfiltration and C2; egress filtering and VPC service controls limit this.
items.how_to_find:
Check for wide-open egress (default allow-all outbound):

```
aws ec2 describe-security-groups --query "SecurityGroups[?IpPermissionsEgress[?IpRanges[?CidrIp=='0.0.0.0/0']]].GroupId"
```
items.how_to_fix: Restrict egress to required destinations (allowlist), route outbound through NAT/proxy with filtering, use VPC endpoints for AWS APIs, and monitor for exfiltration. Re-audit egress rules.

items.title: Flat networks with no tiering
items.description: Workloads of differing trust levels in a single flat subnet allow unrestricted lateral movement after initial access.
items.how_to_find:
Assess whether tiers (web/app/db) are segmented or share one flat network:

```
aws ec2 describe-subnets --query 'Subnets[].[SubnetId,CidrBlock,MapPublicIpOnLaunch]'
```
items.how_to_fix: Segment into tiers (public/private/data subnets) with SG/NACL boundaries and default-deny east-west, place data services in isolated subnets, and use private subnets for compute. Validate segmentation.

items.title: Dangling CNAME to deprovisioned cloud resource
items.description: CNAMEs pointing to released cloud endpoints (S3 website, CloudFront, Azure App Service, Storage, GCP buckets) can be re-registered by attackers to take over the subdomain.
items.how_to_find:
Find CNAMEs pointing at released cloud resources (claimable):

```
subfinder -d target.com -silent | dnsx -cname -resp | grep -Ei 'cloudfront|azurewebsites|github.io|herokuapp|s3'
nuclei -t takeovers/ -l subs.txt
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove dangling DNS records for deprovisioned resources, reclaim/repoint them, and adopt a decommissioning process that deletes DNS before releasing resources. Re-scan for takeovers.

items.title: Takeover of storage/website endpoints
items.description: DNS aliases to S3/GCS static website endpoints or Azure Storage that have been deleted let an attacker create a same-named resource and serve content under the victim domain.
items.how_to_find:
Test storage/static-site endpoints referenced by DNS for claimability:

```
curl -sI https://<name>.s3.amazonaws.com/    # NoSuchBucket => claimable
```
items.how_to_fix: Ensure backing storage/site resources exist and are owned by you, remove references to deleted ones, and monitor for takeover conditions. Re-verify endpoints resolve to owned resources.

items.title: Dangling A record to released elastic/public IP
items.description: A records pointing to released public/elastic IPs can resolve to an attacker who later acquires that IP from the provider pool.
items.how_to_find:
Find A records pointing to IPs you no longer own (attackers can allocate them):

```
dnsx -a -resp -l subs.txt   # compare against your allocated EIPs
```
items.how_to_fix: Remove A records for released IPs, use stable allocations (EIP/reserved IP) only while in use, and delete DNS before releasing IPs. Re-audit A records against owned IPs.

items.title: Public DNS zone information disclosure
items.description: Overly detailed public DNS records (internal hostnames, service identifiers) aid reconnaissance and target selection.
items.how_to_find:
Check for excessive public DNS exposure (internal names, AXFR):

```
dig axfr target.com @<ns>
dnsrecon -d target.com -t std
```
items.how_to_fix: Use split-horizon DNS (don't publish internal names), restrict AXFR to authorized secondaries with TSIG, and minimize public records. Re-test AXFR is refused.

sections.title: Serverless & PaaS — Functions: Privilege, Secrets & Injection

items.title: Over-privileged function execution roles
items.description: Functions running with broad execution roles can be abused for privilege escalation or data access if the function is compromised via injection or a vulnerable dependency.
items.how_to_find:
Review the function's execution role:

```
aws lambda get-function --function-name <f> --query 'Configuration.Role'
aws iam list-attached-role-policies --role-name <role>
```
items.how_to_fix: Scope the execution role to least privilege (specific actions/resources the function uses), one role per function, and remove admin/wildcard policies. Re-audit.

items.title: Secrets in function environment variables
items.description: API keys and credentials stored as function environment variables can be read by anyone with read access to the function configuration.
items.how_to_find:
Inspect function env for plaintext secrets:

```
aws lambda get-function-configuration --function-name <f> --query 'Environment.Variables'
```
items.how_to_fix: Move secrets to Secrets Manager/Parameter Store/Key Vault and fetch at runtime; encrypt env vars with a CMK. Rotate exposed secrets. Re-check env is clean.

items.title: Event-data injection into functions
items.description: Functions triggered by untrusted events (HTTP, queue, object, email) that pass input to commands, queries, or downstream APIs are vulnerable to injection.
items.how_to_find:
Trace event payloads (from queues, uploads, HTTP) into sinks (DB, shell, downstream calls) and test injection:

```
# send crafted event with SQL/command/path payloads and observe behavior
```
items.how_to_fix: Validate/sanitize all event data as untrusted, use parameterized queries, avoid dynamic eval/shell, and enforce schemas. Add injection tests for each trigger. Re-test.

items.title: Publicly invokable functions / function URLs
items.description: Function URLs, public API Gateway routes, or anonymous HTTP triggers without auth let anyone invoke business logic directly.
items.how_to_find:
Check for public function URLs / anonymous invoke:

```
aws lambda get-function-url-config --function-name <f> --query 'AuthType'
```

`NONE` (public) on a sensitive function is a finding.
items.how_to_fix: Require auth (`AWS_IAM`) on function URLs or front with API Gateway + authorizer, restrict resource policy to specific principals, and rate-limit. Re-verify anonymous invoke fails.

items.title: Over-broad function resource policies
items.description: Resource-based policies allowing wildcard principals or arbitrary services to invoke a function widen the trigger surface and may enable cross-account invocation.
items.how_to_find:
Review the function's resource policy for broad invoke grants:

```
aws lambda get-policy --function-name <f> --query Policy --output text | python3 -m json.tool
```

`Principal:*` without `SourceArn`/`SourceAccount` = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Scope invoke permissions to specific sources (add `SourceArn`/`SourceAccount`), remove `*` principals, and grant per-trigger. Re-audit the policy.

items.title: Secrets in code and configuration instead of a vault
items.description: Hardcoded secrets in repos, IaC, and config bypass vault access controls, rotation, and audit logging.
items.how_to_find:
Scan repos/config/images for secrets:

```
gitleaks detect --source . --redact
trufflehog filesystem ./ --only-verified
```
items.how_to_fix: Move all secrets to a managed vault (Secrets Manager/Key Vault/GCP Secret Manager/HashiCorp Vault), reference at runtime, rotate exposed values, and add CI secret scanning + push protection. Re-scan.

items.title: Over-broad access to secret stores
items.description: Wide read access to Secrets Manager, Key Vault, or Secret Manager lets many identities retrieve sensitive material, expanding leak impact.
items.how_to_find:
Review who can read secrets:

```
aws secretsmanager get-resource-policy --secret-id <s>
# who has secretsmanager:GetSecretValue on * ?
cloudsplaining scan ...
```
items.how_to_fix: Scope secret access per-secret to specific roles (least privilege), use resource policies + conditions, separate read from admin, and avoid `GetSecretValue` on `*`. Re-audit.

items.title: Secrets without rotation or expiry
items.description: Long-lived secrets in the vault that are never rotated remain valid indefinitely if leaked.
items.how_to_find:
Check rotation status:

```
aws secretsmanager describe-secret --secret-id <s> --query '[RotationEnabled,LastRotatedDate]'
```

No rotation on long-lived secrets = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Enable automatic rotation (Lambda rotation / Key Vault policy), set sensible lifetimes, and prefer short-lived dynamic credentials (Vault dynamic secrets). Re-check rotation is enabled.

items.title: No auditing on secret access
items.description: Without logging of secret retrieval, abnormal access (e.g., mass reads after a compromise) goes undetected.
items.how_to_find:
Verify secret reads are logged:

```
aws cloudtrail lookup-events --lookup-attributes AttributeKey=EventName,AttributeValue=GetSecretValue --max-results 10
```
items.how_to_fix: Ensure secret-access events are captured in the audit log, alert on anomalous/high-volume reads, and review access. Enable data-event logging where required. Re-verify reads are logged.

sections.title: Logging & Monitoring — Audit Logging & Tamper Resistance

items.title: Audit logging disabled or incomplete
items.description: Missing CloudTrail trails, disabled Azure Activity/diagnostic logs, or absent GCP Data Access logs leave actions unrecorded, crippling detection and forensics.
items.how_to_find:
Check for org-wide, multi-region audit logging:

```
aws cloudtrail describe-trails --query 'trailList[].[Name,IsMultiRegionTrail,IsOrganizationTrail]'
aws cloudtrail get-trail-status --name <t> --query IsLogging
```

Azure: Activity Log + diagnostic settings; GCP: Cloud Audit Logs (Admin+Data).
items.how_to_fix: Enable a multi-region, org-wide trail (all management + relevant data events) to a protected account/bucket; enable Azure diagnostic settings and GCP Data Access logs. Re-verify logging is on everywhere.

items.title: Log tampering / deletion possible
items.description: Trails and log buckets without integrity validation, immutability, or restricted delete permissions can be tampered with to hide attacker activity.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether logs can be altered/deleted (no object-lock/immutability, broad delete perms):

```
aws s3api get-object-lock-configuration --bucket <log-bucket>
aws cloudtrail get-trail --name <t> --query 'Trail.LogFileValidationEnabled'
```
items.how_to_fix: Enable log-file validation, S3 Object Lock (WORM) + MFA-delete on the log bucket, send to a separate restricted logging account, and deny deletes via SCP. Re-verify immutability.

references.title: AWS CloudTrail log file integrity validation

items.title: Data-plane / object access events not logged
items.description: Management-only logging misses S3/object data events, Key Vault secret reads, and storage access, hiding data exfiltration.
items.how_to_find:
Check for data-event logging (object reads/writes, not just control plane):

```
aws cloudtrail get-event-selectors --trail-name <t> --query 'EventSelectors[].DataResources'
```
items.how_to_fix: Enable data events for sensitive buckets/functions (AWS data events, Azure storage logging, GCP Data Access logs), balancing cost, and ship to the SIEM. Re-verify data events appear.

items.title: Insufficient log retention
items.description: Short retention windows prevent investigation of incidents discovered late and may breach compliance requirements.
items.how_to_find:
Check retention against policy/compliance:

```
aws logs describe-log-groups --query 'logGroups[].[logGroupName,retentionInDays]'
```

Short/`null` (never-expire may be fine; too-short is the risk) retention below policy = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Set retention per compliance (e.g. 365+ days for audit logs) with lifecycle to cheaper storage, and archive immutably. Re-check retention meets policy.

items.title: Threat detection service disabled
items.description: GuardDuty, Defender for Cloud, or Security Command Center not enabled across all accounts/regions leaves cloud-native threats undetected.
items.how_to_find:
Check the native detection service is enabled everywhere:

```
aws guardduty list-detectors --query DetectorIds
# Azure: Microsoft Defender for Cloud plans ; GCP: Security Command Center
```
items.how_to_fix: Enable GuardDuty / Defender for Cloud / Security Command Center org-wide across all regions/subscriptions/projects, turn on relevant protection plans, and route findings to a SIEM. Re-verify enabled.

items.title: No alerting / notification on findings
items.description: Detection without alerting (no SNS/EventBridge, Action Group, or Pub/Sub notification) means findings sit unreviewed.
items.how_to_find:
Check whether findings generate alerts/notifications:

```
aws guardduty list-publishing-destinations --detector-id <d>
aws securityhub describe-hub 2>&1
```
items.how_to_fix: Wire findings to SNS/EventBridge/Defender alerts -> ticketing/on-call, define severity-based routing, and test the pipeline. Re-verify high/critical findings page someone.

items.title: Detection feature/coverage gaps
items.description: Detection enabled but with key protections off (e.g., GuardDuty S3/EKS/malware protection, Defender plans per resource type) leaves blind spots.
items.how_to_find:
Identify unenabled detection features (S3/EKS/malware/DNS protection, extra data sources):

```
aws guardduty get-detector --detector-id <d> --query 'Features'
```
items.how_to_fix: Enable all relevant protection plans/data sources (S3, EKS, malware, RDS, Lambda), extend coverage to all accounts/regions, and review gaps periodically. Re-check features enabled.

items.title: No posture management / config baseline
items.description: Without continuous posture management (Security Hub, Defender CSPM, SCC posture), drift from secure baselines goes unnoticed.
items.how_to_find:
Check for CSPM / config compliance:

```
aws configservice describe-configuration-recorder-status
# run Prowler / ScoutSuite for a posture baseline
prowler aws
```
items.how_to_fix: Enable Config/CSPM (Security Hub standards, Defender for Cloud, SCC posture) with a CIS/Foundational baseline, remediate failing controls, and track drift continuously. Re-scan with Prowler to confirm improvement.
